answer:
**Manu on *dharma* as eternal:**

> The entire Veda is the root-source of Dharma (the sacred law and **eternal duty**), as are *Smṛti* (the remembered tradition) and the conduct of those who know it, the practices of the virtuous, and one's own self-satisfaction.[^1]

This verse frames *dharma* (righteousness, sacred law, duty) as rooted in the Veda—itself primordial and unchanging—alongside tradition and virtuous practice, implying a timeless, pre-existing order revealed rather than invented.[^2]

**Charles Comte on moral law (*moeurs*/laws) as conditioned by *époque* (circumstances/epoch) and *peuple* (species/peoples):**

> ...one will thus be able to see how the **moral habits** of each fraction of which the species is composed influence the fate of the whole, and how **migrations, invasions, or conquests transport the ideas, morals, and institutions formed under certain local circumstances, to peoples placed in different circumstances**.[^3]

Comte's Vol. II systematically compares *moeurs* (morals/customs/laws) across human "species" (races/varieties), climates, soils, and epochs, arguing they arise from physical/social conditions (*circonstances locales*), evolve via action/reaction with environment, and shift/transplant with migrations—not from any universal or eternal source.[^4]

**Staging them:** Manu's position is foundationalist/divine-revelationist: *dharma* *always was*, embedded in cosmic order (Veda as root-source). Comte's is historicist/relativist: moral laws are emergent, variable products of peoples (*peuples*, species) and their *époque* (local circumstances like climate, soil, conquests). Manu assumes an unchanging archetype; Comte derives variation empirically from observable differences.

**Which is harder to argue out of?** Comte's. Manu's absolutism yields to counterexamples (e.g., *dharma*'s own *varna*-/epoch-specific adaptations elsewhere in Manu), empirical refutation, or philosophical skepticism about revelation. But Comte's—grounded in cross-cultural data (e.g., islanders' hierarchies varying by latitude/resources)—demands disproving vast observations of moral divergence tied to conditions; rejecting it requires denying evident relativity without a superior causal model.[^5]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 2, [¶p-d70b97](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-d70b97).

[^2]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 2, [whole chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/).

[^3]: *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, Ch. 13, [¶p-a0c9b3](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/13-du-developpement-moral-des-peuples-de-diverses-esp/translation/#p-a0c9b3).

[^4]: *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, Ch. 13, [whole chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/13-du-developpement-moral-des-peuples-de-diverses-esp/translation/); cf. Ch. 6 on climate's indirect role via manners/vices.

[^5]: *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, Ch. 13 passim (e.g., [¶p-b368fa](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/13-du-developpement-moral-des-peuples-de-diverses-esp/translation/#p-b368fa) on circumstance-reaction cycles).
